title-group.article-title: Cardiopulmonary crosstalk in Long COVID: a systematic review of emerging evidence

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long COVID is a complex, multisystem syndrome with significant cardiopulmonary implications. Persistent inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and microvascular injury contribute to prolonged symptoms such as dyspnea, chest pain, and exercise intolerance. Despite growing recognition of these complications, the underlying mechanisms of cardiopulmonary interactions remain poorly understood.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A comprehensive literature search was conducted on PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, and Web of Science covering studies from 2019 to 2025. Keywords included “Long COVID”, “cardiopulmonary interaction”, “pulmonary fibrosis”, “myocardial inflammation”, and “endothelial dysfunction”. A total of 102 articles were included, comprising 65 original research studies and 37 review articles.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Pulmonary sequelae, such as fibrotic remodeling, persistent hypoxia, and microthrombosis, impose significant strain on the cardiovascular system, exacerbating myocardial inflammation, arrhythmias, and endothelial dysfunction. Shared mechanisms, such as oxidative stress, immune dysregulation, and neurohumoral activation, create a vicious cycle of sustained cardiopulmonary impairment. The disruption of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) further contributes to systemic vascular dysregulation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: A deeper understanding of cardiopulmonary interactions in Long COVID is essential for developing effective management strategies. Targeting inflammatory pathways, restoring endothelial function, and addressing autonomic instability may provide therapeutic benefits. As the long-term impact of this syndrome continues to evolve, further research is needed to refine treatment approaches and mitigate its burden on global health.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12872-025-05185-7.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Long COVID refers to persistent symptoms following SARS-CoV-2 infection, affecting about 10% or more of recovering individuals [1]. Within the broader category of post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), Long COVID describes persistent or new-onset symptoms such as fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive impairment, headache, chest pain, exhaustion, shortness of breath, cough, and loss of taste or smell [2]. Initially, COVID-19 was considered primarily a lung disease. However, Long COVID is now recognized as a multi-organ condition. One of the systems most affected is the cardiopulmonary system [3]. COVID-19 can cause both immediate myocardial injury and long-term cardiovascular impairment [4].

Evidence shows that in Long COVID, inflammatory disturbances, fibrotic changes, vascular abnormalities, and microvascular damage disrupt the lung–heart connection. This contributes to persistent symptoms such as shortness of breath, chronic fatigue, and circulatory disorders [3]. The lungs and heart are functionally and pathophysiologically interconnected [5]. A Lung dysfunction increases the hemodynamic load on the heart, while impaired heart function reduces oxygen delivery to the lungs [6].

In Long COVID, chronic inflammation, microvascular injury, and endothelial cell damage intensify this interaction, creating a vicious cycle that can lead to severe cardiopulmonary complications [7]. Chronic hypoxia, cardiac arrhythmias, and blood pressure dysregulation further indicate disruption of this coordination [8].

Given the rising prevalence of Long COVID and the key role of heart–lung interactions in its pathophysiology, understanding these mechanisms is crucial for improving disease management and developing effective treatments. This review explores current evidence on heart–lung connections in Long COVID, the underlying mechanisms, and associated clinical outcomes.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A literature search was performed using PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, and Web of Science databases to identify relevant studies published up to March 2025. The search strategy combined MeSH terms and keywords related to Long COVID and cardiopulmonary interactions, including “Long COVID”, “post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC)”, “cardiopulmonary dysfunction”, “pulmonary fibrosis”, “myocardial inflammation”, “autonomic dysfunction”, “endothelial dysfunction”, “microvascular injury”, “gut-lung-heart axis”, “microbiome dysbiosis”, “oxidative stress”, “mitochondrial dysfunction”, “epigenetic modifications”, “immune dysregulation”, “RAAS activation”, “chronic hypoxia”, and “neurohumoral activation”. This study was conducted as a systematic review according to PRISMA 2020 guidelines, with narrative synthesis (no meta-analysis).

sec.title: Selection process
sec.p: The initial search identified 1,250 records. After removing 200 duplicates, 1,050 records remained for title and abstract screening. Following this screening, 300 articles were selected for full-text review based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Ultimately, 102 articles met all criteria and were included in the final analysis. The study selection process is depicted in the flow diagram (Fig. 1).

caption.p: PRISMA 2020 flow diagram. A total of 1,250 records were identified, 200 duplicates removed, 1,050 records screened by title and abstract, 300 full-text articles assessed for eligibility, and 102 studies included in the final analysis

list-item.p: Peer-reviewed original research articles, clinical studies, and reviews focusing on cardiopulmonary complications in Long COVID.
Studies addressing shared pathophysiological mechanisms between cardiovascular and pulmonary systems in the context of SARS-CoV-2 infection.
Research involving in vitro models, in vivo animal studies, or clinical investigations (case reports, cohort studies, randomized trials).
Articles published in English in recognized scientific journals.
Studies not specifically addressing cardiopulmonary interactions in Long COVID.
Non-peer-reviewed materials such as conference abstracts, commentaries, editorials, and letters to the editor.
Publications in languages other than English.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: All included studies were independently evaluated for methodological quality and risk of bias by two reviewers. Discrepancies were resolved by discussion. Data from each study were extracted independently by the same two reviewers using a standardized data extraction form. Studies with high risk of bias were acknowledged, and their influence on the overall conclusions was considered during data synthesis.

sec.title: Pathophysiology of cardiopulmonary crosstalk in Long COVID
Persistent inflammation and fibrosis
sec.p: A main feature of pulmonary involvement in Long COVID is chronic inflammation and pulmonary fibrosis, both of which can cause lasting impairment of lung function. Chronic inflammation after COVID-19 plays a key role in the persistence and recurrence of symptoms [9]. This process begins in the lung alveoli, where SARS-CoV-2 triggers continuous secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α into surrounding tissues and the bloodstream. The inflammation activates fibroblasts, leading to collagen and fibronectin deposition and, ultimately, fibrosis [10]. SARS-CoV-2 replicates rapidly, infecting type II alveolar epithelial cells (AEC2) and endothelial cells [11]. AEC2 cells are the main epithelial progenitors that can differentiate into type I alveolar epithelial cells (AEC1). Normally, after the initial injury is removed, inflammation subsides, surviving AEC2 cells multiply, and tissue repair occurs. However, when AEC2 function is impaired, these cells recruit myofibroblasts, which produce collagen, induce AEC2 apoptosis, and drive fibrosis. Myofibroblast growth and activation are regulated by multiple factors, including transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1) [12] and platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) [13].

Evidence indicated that pulmonary fibrosis was common after SARS and MERS and now available evidence suggests that SARS-CoV-2 infection may also lead to pulmonary fibrosis [13]. Later, rigidity of the lung tissue will impede gas exchange, with a reduction in lung function, dyspnea, and exercise intolerance [14] (Fig. 2).

caption.p: Pathophysiological mechanisms of pulmonary involvement in Long COVID. A schematic representation showing persistent inflammation, fibrosis, endothelial dysfunction, and microthrombosis contributing to impaired gas exchange and hypoxia

sec.p: Overall, sustained inflammation and pulmonary fibrosis in long COVID can lead to reduced lung function, chronic dyspnea, and decreased exercise tolerance in affected individuals. This highlights the importance of anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic therapeutic strategies in managing these patients.

sec.title: Pulmonary vascular dysfunction and micro thrombosis
sec.p: A major consequence of Long COVID is persistent damage to the pulmonary vasculature and endothelial dysfunction [15]. These changes increase vascular permeability, promote microthrombosis, and reduce pulmonary blood flow [11]. When combined with ongoing inflammation, they contribute to persistent dyspnea and hypoxia [16].

SARS-CoV-2 enters endothelial cells via the ACE2 receptor [16]. Once inside, the virus replicates rapidly, triggering the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-1β (IL-1β), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) [10]. TNF-α and related inflammatory mediators disrupt endothelial function [17], elevate antiplasmin-2 (2AP) levels, and promote a hypercoagulable state, leading to blood microclots resistant to fibrinolysis [16]. Increased soluble intracellular adhesion molecule-1 (sICAM-1) levels [15] and platelet activation [10] further enhance clot formation and drive microthrombogenesis [11].

This hypercoagulable state likely contributes to long-term pulmonary symptoms in Long COVID [11]. Elevated factor VIII [18], D-dimer, and fibrinogen levels have been reported in affected patients [19]. Impaired coagulation–fibrinolysis balance may explain the fibrin deposits observed in the lungs of these patients [19]. Microthrombi can block alveolar capillaries, restrict red blood cell passage, and reduce pulmonary perfusion. In combination with fibrosis-related decreases in effective alveolar volume, this impairs oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange, leading to hypoxia and dyspnea [11] (Fig. 3).

caption.p: Mechanisms of pulmonary vascular dysfunction and microthrombosis in Long COVID. SARS-CoV-2 infects pulmonary endothelial cells via the ACE2 receptor, triggering the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α) and promoting endothelial activation. Increased antiplasmin, sICAM-1, D-dimer, and factor VIII levels contribute to microthrombus formation in alveolar capillaries, resulting in reduced pulmonary blood flow, impaired alveolar volume, and disrupted gas exchange, ultimately leading to hypoxia and dyspnea

sec.p: Microthrombosis also disrupts ventilation–perfusion (V/Q) matching, further impairing gas exchange [20]. Together with chronic inflammation and fibrotic changes, these alterations contribute to reduced oxygenation, persistent hypoxia, and chronic dyspnea in Long COVID patients [21].

Overall, vascular and coagulation abnormalities play a central role in the persistence of pulmonary symptoms in Long COVID. Therapies aimed at preserving endothelial function and modulating coagulation pathways may help alleviate these symptoms.

sec.title: Altered gas exchange and hypoxia
sec.p: One of the major consequences of Long COVID is impaired gas exchange and persistent hypoxia [22]. These abnormalities can cause shortness of breath, reduced exercise tolerance, and systemic effects on multiple organs [23]. They are primarily driven by damage to the alveolar–capillary barrier, reduced oxygen diffusion capacity, and impaired regulation of adaptive responses to hypoxia [24].

SARS-CoV-2 infects alveolar epithelial cells, particularly type II cells, and pulmonary vascular endothelial cells. This leads to severe inflammation, increased vascular permeability, and disruption of the alveolar–capillary barrier. Damage to this barrier allows inflammatory fluid to leak into the alveolar space, impairs surfactant production, and reduces lung elasticity. Together, these changes decrease effective ventilation and oxygen diffusion capacity [25]. Imaging studies of Long COVID patients show that reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO) can persist for months after recovery, confirming ongoing impairment of gas exchange [26].

Persistent hypoxia affects not only the lungs but also other organs, especially the cardiovascular system. Low oxygen levels trigger adaptive responses such as increased erythropoietin production and compensatory polycythemia. However, they can also promote oxidative stress, systemic inflammation, and, in severe cases, heart failure [27]. Some patients develop persistently elevated plasma lactate levels, indicating a shift toward anaerobic glycolysis [28].

In mild to moderate cases, compensatory mechanisms, including increased ventilation and sympathetic activation, raise heart rate and minute ventilation, partially improving oxygen delivery [29]. In severe cases, chronic hypoxia can lead to pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular hypertrophy, and reduced cardiac capacity to compensate for oxygen deficiency [30].

Overall, persistent gas exchange dysfunction and chronic hypoxia are central to the continuation of respiratory symptoms and the decline in quality of life in Long COVID patients. Understanding these mechanisms may help guide targeted treatments to improve gas exchange, reduce lung inflammation, and optimize oxygen delivery.

sec.title: Myocardial inflammation and fibrosis
sec.p: A significant cardiovascular consequence of Long COVID is persistent myocardial inflammation and fibrosis, which can result in cardiac dysfunction, reduced ejection fraction, and an elevated risk of heart failure [31] (Fig. 4). These complications are particularly common in patients who experienced cardiac involvement during the acute phase of COVID-19, and they may persist for months after apparent clinical recovery [32].

caption.p: Cardiovascular complications and mechanisms in Long COVID. A schematic illustration of myocardial inflammation and fibrosis, autonomic dysfunction, endothelial dysfunction, and arrhythmias leading to heart failure

sec.p: Multiple mechanisms contribute to inflammation and myocardial fibrosis in Long COVID [31]. One involves direct SARS-CoV-2 infection of cardiomyocytes via the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor. This interaction can cause cellular injury, necrosis, and activation of local immune responses [33]. In addition, systemic inflammation driven by a hyperactive immune response during acute infection leads to the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β. This persistent inflammatory environment promotes fibroblast activation and extracellular matrix deposition, driving fibrotic remodeling of the myocardium [34].

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in Long COVID patients has demonstrated signs of ongoing inflammation, myocardial edema, and diffuse fibrosis. Increased T2-weighted signal intensity is indicative of inflammation, while late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) suggests fibrosis and necrosis [35]. These structural changes impair myocardial compliance, weaken contractility, and heighten the risk of ventricular arrhythmias [36].

Autoimmune mechanisms may also contribute to the persistence of inflammation and fibrosis. Some patients develop autoantibodies targeting cardiac antigens, resulting in sustained immune-mediated injury to myocardial cells [37]. This pattern is reminiscent of virus-induced inflammatory cardiomyopathy seen in infections such as Coxsackievirus and Parvovirus B19, although in Long COVID the severity and persistence appear more pronounced [38].
Clinically, affected patients may present with shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, or, in advanced cases, reduced left ventricular systolic function and overt heart failure [39].

Overall, persistent myocardial inflammation and fibrosis represent central mechanisms of cardiac involvement in Long COVID. Early detection using cardiac imaging and inflammatory biomarker assessment can facilitate timely intervention and help prevent long-term cardiac sequelae.

sec.title: Autonomic dysfunction and arrhythmias
sec.p: Autonomic dysfunction (dysautonomia) and cardiac arrhythmias are frequent complications in Long COVID. These disorders can cause palpitations, dizziness, blood pressure instability, and impaired cardiovascular responses to stress or physical activity [40].

A common form of dysautonomia in Long COVID is postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) [41]. It is characterized by an excessive rise in heart rate upon standing, often accompanied by weakness, dizziness, fatigue, and brain fog. The underlying mechanisms may include inflammation-related autonomic impairment, baroreceptor dysfunction, and reduced sensitivity of autonomic receptors [42].

SARS-CoV-2 can alter autonomic regulation and disrupt cardiac rhythm through several pathways. One involves direct injury to neural circuits controlling cardiac activity, particularly parasympathetic and sympathetic pathways [43]. Chronic inflammation and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) may also drive sympathetic overactivation and reduce parasympathetic inhibition [44]. This imbalance promotes tachycardia, arrhythmias, and oxidative stress in myocardial tissue [45].

In addition, SARS-CoV-2 may directly or indirectly affect the sinus node and conduction system, leading to arrhythmias [46]. Reported types include inappropriate sinus tachycardia, supraventricular tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, and, in some cases, conduction blocks [47].

Heart rate variability (HRV) studies show that patients with Long COVID and reduced HRV have a higher risk of dysautonomia and arrhythmias [48]. This reflects impaired autonomic control over hemodynamic responses, resulting in excessive sympathetic drive or reduced parasympathetic activity [49].

Autonomic dysfunction and arrhythmias can markedly reduce quality of life in Long COVID. Early recognition allows targeted management, which may include tailored physical activity, medications to control heart rate, and autonomic retraining strategies to enhance cardiovascular stability and reduce symptoms.

sec.title: Endothelial dysfunction and thrombosis
sec.p: Endothelial dysfunction is a major mechanism contributing to cardiovascular complications in Long COVID, leading to a higher risk of thrombosis, vascular inflammation, and impaired blood flow regulation [50]. Under normal conditions, endothelial cells maintain hemodynamic stability, prevent inflammation, and inhibit platelet aggregation by producing nitric oxide (NO), prostacyclin, and other anticoagulant factors. In Long COVID, persistent immune activation, elevated oxidative stress, and direct endothelial injury caused by SARS-CoV-2 impair these functions, creating a prothrombotic state [49].

Patients with Long COVID often exhibit elevated levels of von Willebrand factor (VWF), intercellular adhesion molecule-1 (ICAM-1), and E-selectin, which are markers of endothelial activation. This leads to enhanced platelet adhesion, increased vascular permeability, and sustained vascular inflammation. Reduced NO availability further promotes vasospasm and limits blood flow to vital organs [50].

Evidence also shows persistent hypercoagulability in these patients. Increased fibrinogen, D-dimer, and activated plasminogen levels indicate prolonged coagulation activation and reduced fibrinolysis [51], thereby raising the risk of venous and arterial thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, and other thrombotic complication [52].

Endothelial dysfunction in Long COVID is also linked to chronic fatigue and reduced exercise capacity. Impaired blood flow regulation in skeletal muscles, persistent tissue hypoxia, and reduced oxygen delivery contribute to muscle weakness and physical performance decline [53].

Overall, endothelial dysfunction and sustained thrombotic risk are key drivers of cardiovascular complications in Long COVID, increasing the likelihood of severe outcomes such as stroke, pulmonary embolism, and microvascular disease. Early detection using endothelial biomarkers, coagulation assays, and vascular imaging can improve management and reduce long-term complications.

Pathophysiological mechanisms of cardiopulmonary injury in Long COVID are diverse and complex. Chronic inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, fibrosis, hypercoagulability, and impaired gas exchange are among the key contributors that affect both pulmonary and cardiac systems. These mechanisms lead to a range of clinical consequences such as pulmonary fibrosis, myocarditis, arrhythmias, and exercise intolerance. A detailed summary of these mechanisms and their clinical impacts is provided in Table 1.

caption.p: Pathophysiological mechanisms and clinical consequences of cardiopulmonary injury in long COVID

tr.td: Chronic inflammation
Elevated IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α; fibroblast activation
Persistent inflammation, pulmonary fibrosis, heart failure, arrhythmias
Pulmonary Fibrosis
Collagen deposition, myofibroblast activation, AEC2 dysfunction
Reduced lung compliance, impaired gas exchange
Endothelial Dysfunction and Thrombosis

Venous/arterial thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, exercise intolerance

sec.title: Shared mechanisms linking pulmonary and cardiovascular systems
Chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation
sec.p: One of the hallmark features of Long COVID is persistent immune activation and chronic inflammation, both of which play a central role in long-term cardiovascular and respiratory damage [54]. Prolonged immune hyperactivity results in elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β, often linked to sustained activation of monocytes and macrophages [55].

In addition to monocyte- and macrophage-driven inflammation, mast cells are also implicated in the pathophysiology of Long COVID. SARS-CoV-2 activates toll-like receptors, leading to the production of autoantibodies that interact with mast cell immunoglobulin receptors, causing abnormal and persistent mast cell activation. These cells are major producers of inflammatory cytokines in COVID-19, partly due to their high expression of ACE2 receptors, which facilitates viral entry and amplifies immune responses [54].

Chronic lung inflammation drives the secretion of profibrotic mediators, particularly transforming growth factor beta (TGF-β), a key regulator of pulmonary fibrosis. TGF-β stimulates fibroblasts to upregulate collagen gene expression, leading to stiffening and architectural remodeling of lung tissue. This process reduces pulmonary compliance, impairs gas exchange, and contributes to persistent dyspnea, a common symptom in Long COVID [56].

Cardiac involvement is another consequence of persistent inflammation in Long COVID, potentially resulting in myocarditis, myocardial fibrosis, arrhythmias, and pulmonary hypertension. Although the exact mechanisms behind COVID-19–associated arrhythmias remain unclear, chronic inflammation, direct viral injury, and autoimmune responses to cardiac antigens are likely contributors [57]. Beyond these mechanisms, T cell exhaustion has emerged as a critical feature of immune dysregulation in Long COVID. Studies have reported altered T cell subset distribution, higher frequencies of exhausted SARS-CoV-2–specific CD8 + T cells, and impaired coordination between T and B cell responses. This imbalance between the cellular and humoral arms of adaptive immunity may drive immune dysfunction and symptom persistence [58].

The gut microbiome also plays a pivotal role in shaping immune responses during and after SARS-CoV-2 infection. Dysbiosis, disruption of microbial balance, can increase intestinal permeability, allowing bacterial endotoxins to enter the circulation and exacerbate systemic inflammation. These effects may worsen both cardiac and pulmonary dysfunction. Microbial metabolites, including pro-inflammatory trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) and anti-inflammatory short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), are key mediators along the gut–lung–heart axis [59, 60].

Overall, these immune and inflammatory disturbances not only contribute to prolonged cardiopulmonary symptoms in Long COVID but may also predispose affected individuals to chronic conditions such as heart failure and pulmonary fibrosis. Targeted therapeutic approaches, including immunomodulators, cytokine inhibitors, and microbiome-based interventions, are increasingly being explored to manage and mitigate the long-term consequences of this syndrome [61].

sec.title: Systemic metabolic and oncological sequelae of Long COVID
Dyslipidemia after COVID-19
sec.p: Dyslipidemia after COVID-19 has emerged as a significant metabolic consequence of the infection, reflecting both acute inflammatory injury and long-term metabolic disruption. During acute infection, SARS-CoV-2 triggers a strong inflammatory response that disrupts lipid homeostasis. Pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6 and TNF-α alter hepatic lipid metabolism, increasing triglyceride synthesis and impairing HDL function. In parallel, oxidative stress damages endothelial cells and modifies lipoproteins, further impairing their protective roles [62]. Patients with pre-existing metabolic syndrome, characterized by obesity, hypertension, and diabetes, are especially vulnerable, as their baseline lipid regulation is already compromised [63]. Evidence from meta-analyses indicates that elevated triglycerides and reduced HDL cholesterol during infection are linked to greater disease severity, likely due to their role in amplifying systemic inflammation [64]. This dysregulated lipid profile has also been associated with higher mortality, suggesting that lipids may influence not only the inflammatory process but also immune resolution and organ recovery [65]. Longitudinal population studies reveal that the incidence of new-onset dyslipidemia rose during the pandemic, implying that COVID-19 may act as both a trigger and an accelerator of lipid disorders [66]. Post-acute COVID-19 patients often continue to display lipid abnormalities, sometimes for months, which may contribute to the persistence of fatigue, vascular dysfunction, and cardiovascular risk [67]. Together, these findings point to a multifactorial mechanism in which viral inflammation, oxidative stress, and pre-existing metabolic vulnerabilities converge to produce a sustained dyslipidemic state after COVID-19.

sec.title: Post- COVID hypertension
sec.p: Post-COVID hypertension is emerging as a frequent complication after SARS-CoV-2 infection. The risk of developing new high blood pressure after COVID-19 is higher than after other viral illnesses, including influenza [68]. The exact mechanisms are still unclear, but several pathways are suspected. COVID-19 can cause persistent endothelial dysfunction and activate the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, which may raise blood pressure. In some patients, inflammation and immune dysregulation continue for months, contributing to vascular stiffness and impaired blood pressure regulation [69]. Data from long COVID research show that hypertension-related disorders remain common, even in individuals who did not have hypertension before infection [70]. Comparative studies before and after the pandemic reveal a higher prevalence of hypertension in the post-COVID population, often accompanied by diabetes and other cardiovascular risk factors. Pre-existing hypertension may worsen the course of post-COVID syndrome, leading to more persistent symptoms and poorer sleep quality [71]. These findings suggest that post-COVID hypertension is not simply a short-term complication but may reflect lasting damage to vascular and autonomic regulation.

sec.title: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) and Long COVID
sec.p: T2DM has been reported as both an immediate and long-term complication of COVID-19. The infection can damage pancreatic β-cells directly through viral entry or indirectly via inflammatory mediators. This may lead to impaired insulin secretion and β-cell apoptosis. Persistent inflammation and oxidative stress can worsen insulin resistance, especially in individuals with pre-existing metabolic risk factors [72]. Large cohort studies have shown a higher risk of new-onset T2DM in people recovering from COVID-19 compared with uninfected controls. This increased risk may last for months after the acute phase [73]. In the context of long COVID, diabetes poses additional challenges for diagnosis and management. Symptoms such as fatigue and brain fog can mask early hyperglycemia, delaying diagnosis [74]. Ongoing metabolic monitoring is essential to identify and manage these cases promptly.

sec.title: Cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic (CKM) syndrome and Long COVID
sec.p: Cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic (CKM) syndrome is a newly recognized cluster of interrelated disorders affecting the heart, kidneys, and metabolic systems. Evidence suggests that the prevalence of CKM syndrome increased during the COVID-19 pandemic. SARS-CoV-2 infection may accelerate the development of CKM syndrome by inducing endothelial injury, chronic inflammation, and metabolic dysregulation [75]. In long COVID, persistent inflammation and oxidative stress could worsen pre-existing cardiovascular or kidney disease and impair metabolic control. This can lead to a higher burden of complications such as heart failure, chronic kidney disease progression, and worsening diabetes. Despite these observations, research specifically addressing CKM syndrome in the context of long COVID is still scarce. More longitudinal and mechanistic studies are needed to clarify its pathogenesis and guide prevention strategies.

sec.title: Post-COVID cancer risk
sec.p: The COVID-19 pandemic has raised concerns about potential changes in cancer risk in the post-pandemic period. Delays in cancer screening, diagnosis, and treatment during the pandemic may lead to more late-stage presentations [76]. Safeguarding cancer care in the post-COVID era requires addressing these disruptions and ensuring timely access to oncology services. Reduced access to healthcare, patient hesitancy, and overwhelmed medical systems all contributed to missed or delayed diagnoses [77]. Epidemiological data from the United States show a decline in reported cancer incidences during the pandemic, likely reflecting underdiagnosis rather than a true reduction in cases [78]. This hidden burden could result in increased cancer morbidity and mortality in the coming years. Post-COVID cancer risk may therefore be influenced by both biological effects of infection and indirect impacts on healthcare systems. More research is needed to clarify long-term cancer incidence trends in the post-pandemic world.

sec.title: Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction
sec.p: Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction are key contributors to persistent symptoms and long-term cardiopulmonary damage in Long COVID [79]. During SARS-CoV-2 infection, ACE2 downregulation and RAAS disruption activate NADPH oxidase in endothelial cells, cardiac myocytes, and pulmonary epithelial cells. This promotes excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), driving chronic inflammation, tissue fibrosis, and organ dysfunction [80].

In the heart, high ROS levels combined with weakened antioxidant defenses disrupt the mitochondrial electron transport chain, reducing ATP production and impairing cardiac myocyte function [79, 81]. This mitochondrial injury decreases contractility and promotes oxidative damage to membrane lipids and proteins, predisposing the heart to fibrotic remodeling and heart failure [79]. Oxidative stress may also destabilize the electrical properties of sinoatrial node cells, triggering arrhythmias such as sinus tachycardia and atrial fibrillation [82], both frequently reported in Long COVID [83].

In the lungs, excess ROS damages alveolar epithelial cells, increases vascular permeability, and reduces gas exchange capacity [84]. Mitochondrial dysfunction in pulmonary endothelial cells further impairs nitric oxide (NO) production, a key regulator of vascular homeostasis. Reduced NO bioavailability leads to endothelial dysfunction, impaired vasodilation, increased vascular resistance, and possible development of pulmonary hypertension [85].

Mitochondrial dysfunction also contributes to the metabolic abnormalities and severe fatigue seen in Long COVID. Evidence suggests reduced mitochondrial electron transport chain activity and impaired β-oxidation of fatty acids, limiting the energy supply needed for optimal cardiac and muscle performance. This energy deficit likely explains the profound fatigue and poor exercise tolerance in these patients, similar to patterns observed in heart failure, where mitochondrial dysfunction is also central [79].

sec.title: Microvascular injury and impaired oxygen delivery
sec.p: Endothelial dysfunction is a major pathophysiological feature of Long COVID. It reduces tissue oxygen delivery and affects both the pulmonary and cardiovascular systems. This dysfunction likely contributes to persistent symptoms such as dyspnea, fatigue, and reduced exercise tolerance.

SARS-CoV-2 has complex effects on the endothelium and coagulation pathways. It causes vascular injury and promotes clot formation in both large vessels and small capillaries. Traditional models of vascular disease emphasize large-vessel obstruction and ischemia. However, new evidence shows that uneven capillary transit times also play a central role in oxygen transport. When blood flow across the capillary bed becomes irregular, the surface available for oxygen diffusion decreases. This reduces oxygen exchange even if overall perfusion appears normal. Autopsy studies of patients who died from COVID-19 respiratory failure reveal many more alveolar capillary microthrombi than are seen in influenza A–related ARDS. COVID-19 lungs also show intussusceptive angiogenesis—a disorganized form of new vessel growth, likely triggered by tissue hypoxia. These new vessels are often structurally weak and may create shunts that bypass oxygen exchange [86].

‏ Compared with classical ARDS, this shunting is more pronounced in severe COVID-19 and is associated with lower arterial oxygenation. The thin alveolar–capillary barrier, designed for efficient gas diffusion, becomes damaged by inflammation, swelling, and cellular debris. This damage impairs oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide clearance, returning poorly oxygenated blood to the circulation and worsening hypoxia. Endothelial dysfunction in COVID-19 is not limited to the Lungs. In a prospective study of low-risk individuals, more than 70% showed damage in at least one organ, and nearly 30% had multi-organ involvement. Affected organs included the heart, kidneys, liver, pancreas, and spleen [61]. These findings highlight the systemic nature of endothelial dysfunction and its central role in the long-term effects of COVID-19.

sec.title: Gut-lung-heart axis and the role of microbiome
sec.p: In addition to research on the pathophysiology and treatment of COVID-19, increasing attention has been given to microbiome dysbiosis in the gut, oral cavity, nasopharynx, and lungs. These microbial imbalances are linked to both disease susceptibility and progression. Given the uncertain long-term efficacy of current vaccines and the limited number of pharmacologic options, the microbiome represents a promising target for new interventions [87].

Recent studies suggest that microbiome disruption can drive systemic inflammation, immune dysfunction, and loss of homeostasis, features typical of severe COVID-19. Zuo et al. reported gut dysbiosis in COVID-19 patients, marked by reduced beneficial microbes and enrichment of opportunistic pathogens. These changes persisted even after viral clearance and were correlated with disease severity and inflammatory marker levels [87]. Gut permeability may also increase during infection, producing a “leaky gut” that allows microbial products and inflammatory mediators such as IL-6 to enter the circulation, amplifying the inflammatory response [88, 89].

The widespread expression of ACE2, the viral entry receptor, across multiple organs, including the lungs, gut, liver, heart, and kidneys, may explain the multi-organ involvement seen in COVID-19. Loss of ACE2 function disrupts RAAS regulation, promoting inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and cardiovascular injury [87].

Microbial metabolites such as trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO), produced by gut bacteria during choline metabolism, have also gained attention. Elevated TMAO levels are associated with endothelial dysfunction, platelet hyperreactivity, and thrombotic events, frequent complications in severe COVID-19. In addition, scavenger receptor B1 (SR-B1), which facilitates cholesterol transport and may aid viral entry, can be upregulated by TMAO, potentially increasing SARS-CoV-2 infectivity [90].

Overall, these findings suggest a mechanistic link between gut microbial imbalance, systemic inflammation, immune dysregulation, and cardiopulmonary pathology. Understanding this gut–lung–heart axis may open new possibilities for microbiome-targeted therapies to prevent or reduce the risk of Long COVID.

A comparative analysis of cardiac and pulmonary injuries reveals overlapping yet distinct pathological features. While both organs are affected by chronic inflammation and oxidative stress, specific mechanisms such as microvascular injury and gut microbiome dysbiosis have varying degrees of influence.

sec.title: Epigenetic modifications and long-term disease trajectory
sec.p: Emerging evidence highlights the important role of immune–metabolic interactions in shaping the long-term course of COVID-19. Malnutrition and systemic inflammation, especially during respiratory infections such as COVID-19, can weaken anti-inflammatory pathways and impair immune regulation. This drives organ crosstalk, particularly between the lungs and other systems, through mechanisms beyond direct viral injury [91].

While the acute phase of COVID-19 is well studied, the processes that cause long COVID are less understood. Epigenetic changes, including DNA methylation, histone modifications, chromatin remodeling, and non-coding RNAs, may help explain symptom persistence despite viral clearance. These changes alter gene expression without changing the DNA sequence and can last for long periods, sustaining pathological immune responses [92].

Mitochondrial dysfunction is a recognized hallmark of long COVID. Patients often show impaired oxidative phosphorylation, reduced ATP production, and altered expression of genes involved in mitochondrial metabolism. These abnormalities may explain symptoms such as fatigue, brain fog, and muscle weakness. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) has revealed metabolic disturbances in the muscles and brain of patients with long COVID, consistent with mitochondrial impairment [79].

Defective mitochondria not only reduce cellular energy but also generate excess reactive oxygen species (ROS), causing oxidative stress. This damages DNA, lipids, and proteins, fueling a cycle of inflammation and tissue injury. Similar mitochondrial deficits occur in heart failure, a condition that shares symptoms like fatigue and exercise intolerance, suggesting a shared pathological mechanism centered on energy metabolism. Beyond their role in ATP production, mitochondria regulate immune responses. Their dysfunction can activate inflammatory pathways such as NF-κB and the NLRP3 inflammasome, contributing to the chronic, low-grade inflammation reported in long COVID [79, 93–95].

Chronic inflammation and metabolic disruption are closely linked. Key metabolites from glycolysis and the TCA cycle, such as lactate, succinate, and citrate, act as immune signals. Depending on the context, they can push immune cells toward pro- or anti-inflammatory states. Long COVID appears to involve a lasting metabolic shift toward glycolysis, the “Warburg effect”, which sustains immune activation but reduces energy efficiency [96].

High levels of glucose and mannose have been associated with severe COVID-19. Disruption of glycolysis and glutaminolysis can limit viral replication, suggesting that SARS-CoV-2 hijacks host metabolism to support its life cycle. Patients with long COVID often show signs of metabolic acidosis, mitochondrial injury, and high lactate levels, patterns similar to those seen in severe bacterial sepsis and multi-organ dysfunction [97, 98].

Overall, long COVID may represent a chronic, self-sustaining condition driven by a metabolic–epigenetic feedback loop. At its center is mitochondrial dysfunction, which generates ROS, drives inflammation, and disrupts metabolic regulation. Breaking this cycle, through targeted interventions such as caloric restriction, physical activity, or drugs that enhance mitochondrial function, may help restore immune and metabolic balance. Complementary anti-inflammatory therapies could also help resolve persistent symptoms [99].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Long COVID manifests as a systemic condition, yet the heart is particularly vulnerable. Emerging data show that myocarditis, a serious inflammation of the heart muscle, is significantly linked to SARS-CoV-2 infection. Large-scale analysis indicates that patients with COVID-19 have nearly 16 times the risk of developing myocarditis compared to those without infection [100]. This risk is especially pronounced in the youngest and oldest age groups and underscores the need for vigilant monitoring across demographics.

The pathophysiology of COVID-19-associated myocarditis is believed to involve a combination of direct viral injury to cardiomyocytes and immune-mediated damage from the host response. Clinically, patients may present with ECG changes and elevated cardiac biomarkers, and imaging (such as cardiac MRI or CT) is essential to characterize myocardial inflammation and exclude coronary artery disease [101].

From a clinical standpoint, this emphasizes the need for integrated evaluation of long COVID patients presenting with cardiovascular symptoms, particularly chest discomfort, palpitations, or unexplained fatigue. Following the ACC’s guidance, such patients should undergo cardiology assessment, including imaging and biomarker studies, before resuming physical activity, with athletes requiring especially cautious staging [102].

Moreover, the overlapping mechanisms, systemic inflammation, endothelial injury, mitochondrial dysfunction, likely contribute to cardiac sequelae and should be addressed in a holistic treatment strategy. For example, therapies targeting inflammation, oxidative stress, and metabolic dysregulation, combined with tailored rehabilitation, may mitigate the risk of myocarditis and its consequences.

To strengthen the manuscript, we have now incorporated clinical evidence on myocarditis incidence and mechanism, and emphasized the importance of a clinically translatable approach, integrating direct diagnostic strategies and management recommendations.

For policy, the evidence suggests that guidelines should recommend monitoring and rehabilitation programs addressing both cardiac and pulmonary sequelae of Long COVID. For future research, studies should focus on identifying predictors of cardiopulmonary complications, evaluating targeted therapies for myocarditis and systemic inflammation, and conducting long-term follow-up to understand the trajectory of Long COVID.

This review has some limitations. The included studies were very different in design, population, and outcomes. Many studies had small sample sizes and short follow-up. We did not perform any quantitative analysis.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Long COVID is more than a respiratory aftereffect, it is a system-wide syndrome that notably impacts the heart. Myocarditis stands out as a serious complication, with COVID-19 increasing its risk by an order of magnitude [100]. The inflammation-driven damage to the myocardium, combined with immune response and endothelial disruption, demands integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches rather than symptom-by-symptom management.

Clinicians should be proactive in assessing cardiac involvement in long COVID, using imaging and biomarkers to identify myocarditis early. Treatment strategies should aim not only to alleviate symptoms but to restore cardiorespiratory function and energy homeostasis. Future research must refine risk predictors, evaluate targeted therapies, and promote multidisciplinary collaboration across cardiology, immunology, and rehabilitation specialties.
By marrying mechanistic insights with patient-centred care, we move toward genuine recovery, turning the complex cascade of long COVID into a path toward healing.
